coredata.dc:description: As an excellent fluorescent nanomaterial, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have the properties of small molecular weight, small particle size, high fluorescence stability, no optical flicker, wide excitation spectrum, continuous, long adjustable emission wavelength, good biocompatibility and low toxicity. At present, it has been widely used in biological detection, in vivo and in vitro imaging, drug delivery and diagnostic drugs. This review presents a broad overview of recent progress in synthesis, optical properties, and biomedical applications of CQDs. The synthesis methods of CQDs are first introduced, followed by an extensive discussion of its purification, surface functionalization methods and stability. Furthermore, the optical properties of CQDs are presented, with an emphasis on photoluminescence mechanism and the relationship between synthesis conditions and optical properties. This review's much focus is given to the biomedical applications of CQDs, including detection, imaging (in vivo and in vitro imaging) and drug delivery (targeted therapy, photodynamic and photothermal therapy, and immunological and gene therapy). Finally, we cover the key issues and future prospects/advances that CQDs still have to deal with in the biomedical sector as well as possible solutions.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As an excellent fluorescent nanomaterial, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have the properties of small molecular weight, small particle size, high fluorescence stability, no optical flicker, wide excitation spectrum, continuous, long adjustable emission wavelength, good biocompatibility and low toxicity. At present, it has been widely used in biological detection, in vivo and in vitro imaging, drug delivery and diagnostic drugs. This review presents a broad overview of recent progress in synthesis, optical properties, and biomedical applications of CQDs. The synthesis methods of CQDs are first introduced, followed by an extensive discussion of its purification, surface functionalization methods and stability. Furthermore, the optical properties of CQDs are presented, with an emphasis on photoluminescence mechanism and the relationship between synthesis conditions and optical properties. This review's much focus is given to the biomedical applications of CQDs, including detection, imaging (in vivo and in vitro imaging) and drug delivery (targeted therapy, photodynamic and photothermal therapy, and immunological and gene therapy). Finally, we cover the key issues and future prospects/advances that CQDs still have to deal with in the biomedical sector as well as possible solutions.

ce:keyword.ce:text: In vitro and in vivo imaging

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon Quantum Dots are a family of zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials with exceptional fluorescence capabilities. CQDs consist of ultra-fine, distributed, quasi-spherical carbon nanoparticles smaller than 10 nm in size (Fig. 1 ). It not only overcomes some shortcomings of traditional organic dyes and nanomaterials, but also has superior performance than traditional semiconductor quantum dots, including small molecular weight, low toxicity, simple surface functionalization, diverse sources of raw materials, low cost, tiny particle size, excellent fluorescence stability, adjustable emission wavelength and strong biocompatibility [1].

Depending on the link between the carbon source and the result, the preparation methods for CQDs can be categorized into two preparation styles: “top-down” and “bottom-up”. The top-down approach uses physical or chemical methods to convert large sp2 carbon domains into small CQDs, mainly including chemical oxidation, laser ablation [2], arc discharge [3], and electrochemical synthesis [4]. The bottom-up method uses small molecules as carbon source precursors, and forms sp2 carbon domains through the coupling of the hydrothermal method, microwave-assisted method, and pyrolysis method, resulting in controllable size and morphology. of CQDs.

UV–vis absorption and photoluminescence are characteristics of CQDs. Its UV absorption properties can be altered by surface passivation or surface functionalization, enhancing its absorption peak or red-shifting its absorption wavelength [5,6]. At the same time, surface passivation, doping, altering the particle size, and surface functionalization can increase the fluorescence intensity by boosting the quantum yield of CQDs. Photoluminescence is the most distinctive and fascinating optical characteristic of carbon dots. Photoluminescence is a phenomenon in which carbon dots obtain energy through external light irradiation and their energy levels transition to emitting fluorescence. The specific mechanism for carbon dots' photoluminescence has not yet been proposed by researchers. The two main light-emitting processes now in use are the surface defects of CQDs and the band gap transition of conjugated π domains [7].

Here, we first discuss the various methods of producing and purification CQDs before going into great detail on their optical properties and the process of photoluminescence. The development of CQDs in the biomedical sector is highlighted in the final section, particularly the development in the three distinct disciplines of bioanalytical detection, bioimaging, and drug delivery. The biomedical uses of CQDs for drug delivery, in vitro and in vivo imaging, bioanalytical detection, and other purposes are outlined.

The top-down method is to use physical or chemical methods to convert large sp2 carbon domains into small-sized CQDs, mainly including chemical oxidation, laser ablation [2], arc discharge [3], and electrochemical synthesis [4]. The process is quick, easy, and efficient, but the precursor is only compatible with materials like graphene, carbon fiber, and carbon black that have large sp2 carbon domains [30]. The method cannot precisely control the product size and morphological distribution. But it provides an opportunity to develop CQDs with tunable emission wavelengths due to the diversification of size and morphology.

The method of chemical oxidation is to oxidize carbon source precursor materials with oxidants to obtain CQDs [31]. This method does not require sophisticated equipment for mass production. The carbon-based quantum dots have high hydrophilicity and chemistry due to the abundance of oxygen-containing functional groups (such as –OH and –COOH) that are produced on their surface during the oxidation process, which endows CQDs with excellent hydrophilicity and chemical activity.

Monodisperse water-soluble CQDs were synthesized by chemical oxidation of flour, with spherical particles with a particle size of about 5–8 nm13. Similarly, Luo prepared CQDs from loofah sponge-based activated carbon fibers by chemical oxidation [15]. Soni also used palm shell powder (PSP) and trifluoromethanesulfonic acid to prepare N and S co-doping CQDs which can be used as fluorescent probes for nitrophenols (NPs) [16].

This method is continuously irradiating a carbon source with lasers of different wavelengths, exfoliating carbon nanoparticles from the carbon source under high temperature and high pressure, and obtaining carbon dots after purification [32]. Although this approach is straightforward and practical, the uniformity of the particle size and the equipment requirements make it unsuitable for industrial production. This significantly restricts the use of CQDs. Using pulsed laser ablation, Ren created N-doped microporous CQDs (NM-CQDs) from sustainable biomass with a high quantum yield (32.4%) as well as dual-wavelength photoluminescence (PL) emission. Additional research revealed that NM-CQDs' emission behavior remained independent and stable throughout a range of excitation wavelengths, salt ion concentrations, pH levels, irradiation times, and temperatures. The resulting NM-CQDs demonstrated good internalization in several cells and were well suited for cell labeling pictures thanks to their robust and stable PL emission [9].

LeCroy used the arc discharge method to treat the graphite rod to produce carbon black, then ultrasonically dispersed the carbon black to produce carbon nanoparticles, then oxidized the carbon nanoparticles with nitric acid, followed by dialysis and purification to produce CQDs, and finally used 2,2'-(methylenedioxy)diethylamine to passivate the surface of the CQDs, which had strong fluorescence [17]. CQDs obtained by the arc discharge method have good fluorescence, but the particle size of CQDs is not uniform, and the yield is extremely low, which is not suitable for mass production [33].

This method usually applies a certain voltage or current to the working electrode (carbon material with conductive properties) to make the anode undergo an oxidation reaction, so that the carbon nanoparticles are peeled off from the carbon source, and carbon dots are obtained after centrifugation [5]. The advantages of this method are low cost, easy operation, CQDs with tunable emission wavelengths can be prepared under different synthesis conditions, and there is still a lot of room for the preparation of long-wavelength emission and multicolor CQDs. Li synthesized CQDs by electrochemically oxidizing graphite rods, but only used distilled water as the electrolyte during the experiment [18]. Ran also prepared bimodal emitting carbon dots (B-CDs) from low-cost carbon sources, including graphite and raw coal, by a heat-assisted electrochemical strategy, which exhibited bimodal light at 415 and 510 nm under single-wavelength excitation luminescence (PL) emission [19].

In this method, small molecules are used as carbon source precursors, and organic molecules are coupled to form sp2 carbon domains by hydrothermal method, microwave-assisted method, pyrolysis method, etc., thereby forming CQDs with controllable size and morphology. The advantages of this approach include reasonably uniform size, tunable morphology, green and abundant raw material sources, low cost, and time-consuming and intricate.

This process, which primarily involves the pyrolysis of organic matter to cause it to carbonize and produce fluorescent CQDs under high pressure and temperature, is the favored one for creating CQDs that are environmentally friendly.

The hydrothermal method of making carbon dots was first described in 2011, and it involved dissolving monopotassium phosphate (KHPO+) and glucose in deionized water in a certain molar ratio. Centrifugation and freeze-drying were done after the reaction, which was conducted in a closed autoclave chamber lined with polytetrafluoroethylene. The benefit of this process is that both the surface preparation and the creation of carbon dots are done simultaneously. Additionally, by varying the KHPO+ concentration, carbon dots' luminous characteristics can be tailored. The synthesized Cdots changed from blue to green when activated by UV light as the molar ratio of glucose/KHPO+ increased. The average diameter of the synthetic carbon dots also altered as a result, according to TEM analysis [21]. So far, carbon dots with emission wavelengths covering the entire visible light range can be synthesized by selecting appropriate organic molecules and hydrothermally tuning the reaction environment (such as basic or acidic) with controlled surface functional groups [32]. A system for the hydrothermal aqueous mesophase pitch (AMP)-based synthesis of fluorescent CQDs with high quantum yield (QY) was also developed by Cheng. The prepared CQDs (CQDs-120-24) were maintained at 120 °C for 24 h, and the QY may reach 27.6% [10].

In addition to glucose, natural biological resources [22](various vegetables, fruits, plant leaves, juices, spices, etc.) and waste contaminants [34] are also processed to synthesize carbon dots by hydrothermal synthesis at lower temperatures. Numerous hydrophilic functional groups, including –OH and –COOH, which provide C dots good water solubility, were found by XPS and FTIR investigation. This method does not require expensive chemicals or surface post-passivation treatments. Hence, this study has inspired ongoing research towards the one-step synthesis of carbon dots using biological resources.

The hydrothermal method is a bottom-up synthesis method that is widely used at present. It is environmentally friendly, the process is easy to control, and the reaction yield is relatively high. However, because it needs to be carried out under high temperature and high pressure reaction conditions, the reaction process needs to be carefully monitored, and how to ensure safety is a focus that needs attention. In addition, the production and surface preparation of carbon quantum dots are carried out simultaneously, and the size control of CQDs is not satisfactory.

Guo prepared nitrogen-doped CQDs by a microwave-assisted method using Glu and the amino groups of MPD as precursors and ethylene glycol as a solvent by heating small organic molecules at 120 °C for 5 min25. The CQD created in this way fluoresced green when exposed to 365 nm ultraviolet light. Similarly, López ultrasonically mixed the lactose solution with concentrated hydrochloric acid and placed it in a polytetrafluoroethylene reaction vessel, treated it with hydrothermal microwaves at 160 °C for 15 min, and then the resulting product was centrifuged, filtered, pH adjusted and dialyzed to finally obtain fluorescent CQDs [25]. The microwave-assisted method provides a new route for the preparation of CQDs, which has the advantages of convenient and controllable operation, effectively providing rapid and uniform heat for the reaction, cutting down on reaction time, and improving the yield and quantum yield of CQDs [35–37].

Although the microwave-assisted method is relatively simple and time-saving, this method is only suitable for small-scale production at present, and only by solving this problem can it be truly applied to actual production. In addition, in the synthesis process, the microwave-assisted method directly performs hydrothermal microwave treatment on the reaction solution, because there is no way to effectively control the size of the synthesized CQDs under this condition.
The process involves carbonizing and pyrolyzing organic small molecules at a specific temperature to produce luminous carbon dots.

The scientific team of Emmanuel P. Giannelis [27] made the initial discovery of pyrolysis. According to studies, various ammonium citrate salts are employed to create carbon dots, with the organic ammonium serving as a surface modification and the citrate serving as the carbon source. By choosing the appropriate surface modifiers, organic and hydrophilic carbon dots can be produced. The research proved that the amide function of CQDs, which regulates the generation of reactive oxygen species, was inherited from surface proteins of bacterial origin. In addition to citrate, other small molecules have also been used to synthesize carbon dots by pyrolyses, such as EDTA-synthesized polymers, epoxy-enriched polystyrene, and natural substances (hair, tea leaves, konjac flour, red pepper, etc.) [28].

Wu also placed six bacterial strains in a crucible by carbonization pyrolysis and heated them at 200–220 °C for 2 h32. After cooling at room temperature for 3 h, and ultrasonically treating them in water for 2 h, CQDs were obtained. The research proved that the amide function of CQDs, which regulates the generation of reactive oxygen species, was inherited from surface proteins of bacterial origin.

The carbonization pyrolysis method uses organic molecules or polymers for carbonization pyrolysis. An important challenge of this method is that due to the aggregation of carbon in the synthesis process, the size and structure of the synthesized CQDs cannot be precisely and effectively controlled, resulting in carbon quantum dots. Points are polydisperse.

The separation and purification of CQD is of great significance for achieving quality control of biological systems. Commonly used purification methods include electrophoresis, centrifugation, dialysis, column chromatography.

The electrophoresis method is to achieve separation from each other different moving distances due to different charges or charge-to-mass ratios after electrophoresis for a certain period of time. The first CQDs discovered were purified by electrophoresis [38]. Its advantage is that the purification effect is good, and the defect is more cumbersome.

The centrifugation method is to remove large particles of impurities in the solution through low-speed centrifugation and other operations, and then ultracentrifugation separates the small-sized carbon dots and the solution. It is widely used in the purification of carbon dots due to its simple and easy operation. However, the size of the carbon dots is usually less than 10 nm, and conventional centrifugation is difficult to achieve the separation accuracy requirements, so it is often used with other methods. Jia remove large particles or pellets in solution by centrifuging the samples at 10,000 rpm for 15 min, and subsequently, N-CDs are obtained by further purification by dialysis [26].

The dialysis method uses the principle of diffusion pressure to make the large molecular weight biomolecules in the sample solution trapped in the dialysis bag, while the salt and small molecule substances continue to diffuse dialysis to the outside of the dialysis bag until the concentration inside and outside the dialysis bag reaches a balance. According to the molecular weight of the raw material, dialysis bags with different molecular weight interception sizes are selected, often used in conjunction with centrifugation. It has the characteristics of high separation efficiency, easy operation and low energy consumption. However, there are disadvantages such as high cost and inability to separate large nanostructure by-products. And because the carbon point has good hydrophilicity, it is generally necessary to remove impurities for 24 or 48 h, which consumes a lot of time and distilled water, and is not economical. Guo added the synthesized MPD-Glu CQDs mixture to a dialysis bag (cut-off molecular weight> 3500 Da), dialyzed for 24 h, and removed excess precursors and ethylene glycol molecules [12].

Column chromatography is mainly based on the different adsorption force of substances on silica gel to achieve the purpose of separation of each component. It can realize the separation and transformation of carbon points to obtain the target carbon points. However, compared with other methods, it has the disadvantages of cumbersome operation process, low separation efficiency, difficulty in achieving continuous operation, and difficulty in providing high-purity carbon dots in a short time. LeCroy first obtained precursor carbon nanoparticles by dialysis and centrifugation, followed by further purification of functionalized EDA-CQDs by centrifugation and gel column [17].

In general, whether it is electrophoresis, centrifugation, dialysis or column chromatography, it has its own advantages and disadvantages. Therefore, according to the different research content and needs, different purification methods can be selected to obtain better purification results. It can also be combined by different means such as filtration, dialysis, column chromatography, etc.

It is necessary to study the photostability, biodegradability, and in vivo stability of CQDs, which are essential for biological imaging. In order to study the biodegradability of CQDs, Tao labeled 125I atoms on the surface of CQDs (Cdots-M) prepared by using multi-walled carbon nanotubes as raw materials to obtain 125I-Cdots-M. Then they investigated the distribution and metabolism of quantum dots in female BALB/c mice after intravenous injection of 125I-Cdots-M. The results showed that 125I-Cdots-M mainly accumulated in the reticuloendothelial system—liver and spleen, and also distributed in other organs. The concentration was highest after 5 h of exposure, and the concentration generally decreased after 7 days. It showed that mice can excrete CQDs through normal metabolic pathways. Under the injection dose of 20 mg/kg, the test mice did not show any toxicity such as death, weight loss, abnormal blood biochemical indexes for 90 days [39].

Excellent photostability ensures that CQDs can be continuously tracked for long periods of time as immunofluorescent labeled tissues and cells [40]. Hua evaluated the photostability of CQDs with intrinsic mitochondrial targeting ability after synthesis. Studies have shown that fluorescence intensity exhibits only small fluctuations in the range of 560–805 nm at pH < 13. On the other hand, comparing CQDs with MitoTracker showed that in MCF-7 cells, the fluorescence intensity of CQD dropped to 80% after continuous irradiation for 30 min, but the fluorescence intensity of Mito Tracker was only 20% [41]. The results show that CQDs have excellent photostability and are favorable conditions as probes.

As a popular material for in vivo bioimaging, carbon quantum dots need to maintain their stability in vivo. Yang injected C-Dots solution into mice and used an in vivo imaging system to observe them. They found that the C-Dots injected into mice diffused slowly, and the fluorescence began to weaken 24 h after injection [42]. This confirms that C-Dots have better stability in vivo.

Since there are reaction sites on the surface of CQDs, it can be functionalized on the surface, so that carbon quantum dots have high selectivity, excellent optical properties and other characteristics, so that they can be widely used in various fields [43–45]. The surface functionalization of carbon quantum dots can be divided into covalent and non-covalent methods.

The covalent method is a commonly used surface functionalization method. Liao synthesized CQDs by hydrothermal method using EDTA·2Na as raw material, and then used spiropyran to functionalize the surface of CQDs. When the prepared spiropyran functionalized CQDs are irradiated with ultraviolet light, the fluorescence at 510 nm will gradually weaken, while the fluorescence at 650 nm will gradually increase. When illuminated with visible light, this is now reversed, with a gradual decrease in fluorescence at 650 nm and a gradual increase in fluorescence at 510 nm. At the same time, they conducted multiple tests on the reversibility of their photoluminescence. The experimental results showed that the photoreversibility of spiropyran-functionalized CQDs will not produce obvious “fatigue” effect and can be used repeatedly. Such photoreversibility and stability enable spiropyran-functionalized QDs to be applied in various aspects in the biological field [45]. Su also performed surface functionalization of Iodine doped CQD(I-CQD) after their synthesis by a facile one-pot hydrothermal method. They coupled targeting molecules cetuximab to I-CQDs to prepare I-CQDs-C225. Afterwards, I-CQDs-C225 was subjected to cell imaging, and the results showed that I-CQDs-C225 could be effectively internalized into HCC827 cells and mainly located in the lysosomes of the cells. Therefore, I-CQDs-C225 can be used as an effective probe for fluorescence/CT doublet imaging [46].

Non-covalent methods are mainly through coordination, π-π interaction, hydrogen bonding and other forms. Liu synthesized CQD by hydrothermal method using citric acid, aminoacetic acid and phosphorus pentoxide as raw materials. Then the surface of the CQD is coated with NH2-PEG-NH2, and then the anti-VEGF aptamer is activated under the action of the coupling agent EDC/NHS, thereby forming the CQD-IR820-Aptamer nanocomposite. The results of cell experiments on CQD-IR820-Aptamer showed that in addition to improving the phototherapeutic effect on hypoxic tumors, this quantum dot can also synergistically perform type I PDT (PDTI), type II PDT (PDTII) and photothermal therapy (PTT) [47]. In view of the lack of tumor targeting and low penetration efficiency of ordinary CQD, Yang used physical modification to bind the tumor homing penetrating peptide iRGD (CRGDKGPDC) to CQD. The prepared iRGD-CQDs have good serum stability and low hemolysis rate, can specifically bind to αvβ3 integrin receptors, and have better tumor imaging effects [48]. Liu prepared FA-CQD by utilizing the hydrogen bond interaction between folic acid (FA) and CQD after CQD was synthesized by microwave-assisted hydrothermal carbonization. FA-CQD is able to pinpoint folate receptor-positive cancer cells in a mixture of cells, enabling fluorescent targeted imaging of cancer cells [49].

Carbon dots (CDs) often exhibit substantial ultraviolet (230–400 nm) absorption, and some absorption band tails even reach into the visible spectrum [50,51]. The C–C bond's π-π∗ transition is responsible for the characteristic peak at 260–320 nm, and the CO bond's n-π∗ transition is responsible for the shoulder peak at 270–390 nm. Surface passivation or surface functionalization can change the UV absorption characteristics of carbon dots by boosting their absorption peaks or red-shifting their absorption wavelengths [52]. The absorption spectrum of some carbon dots is a slope-shaped curve without an obvious absorption peak [53].

Quantum yield and fluorescence intensity in CQDs are correlated. The intensity of the fluorescence increased with the quantum yield. Surface passivation [54] and doping [55] are the key determinants of quantum yield. In addition, the fluorescence of CQDs is somewhat influenced by particle size, pH level, and surface functional groups [56].

CQDs' fluorescence intensity can be increased by surface passivation [57], and this fluorescence intensity increases as the amount of surface passivation agent increases. The intensity of the fluorescence stops increasing once it reaches a specific point. So far, the main passivating agents are organic molecules and polymers. After surface passivation using 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethyl aminopropyl)-carbodiimide (EDC), which significantly increased the fluorescence intensity. With the increase of EDC concentration from 2.5 to 25 mM, the QY of surface passivated CQDs increased from 18.83% to 41.10% [58]. Omer prepared highly passivated green phosphorus-nitrogen co-doped CQDs by low-temperature carbonization with a fluorescence quantum yield of 25%. In addition, CQD as a fluorophore can detect Cu2+ ions down to 1.5 nM [59]. Besides, Wang synthesized CQDs with strong fluorescence by a solvothermal method using anhydrous citric acid as carbon precursor and 1-hexadecyl amine (HDA) as surface passivator, with a quantum yield as high as 53% [60].

Doping is another efficient way to adjust the fluorescence emission wavelength and raise quantum yield in addition to surface passivation [61,62]. Anilkumar used inorganic salts (ZnS and TiO2) to separately dope the cores of CQDs, while their surfaces were passivated with polyethylene glycol diamine. Compared with pure surface passivation, the fluorescence quantum yield of CQDs doped with inorganic salts and surface passivation was greatly improved. ZnS-doped CQDs had a 78% quantum yield, while TiO2-doped CQDs had a 70% quantum yield [63]. In addition to doping inorganic salts, the fluorescence quantum yield of CQDs can also be improved by doping other elements, including nitrogen, sulfur [64,65], phosphorus [66–68], boron [69], fluorine [70]and other nonmetals. Citric acid and thiourea were utilized as reaction precursors in a hydrothermal approach to produce nitrogen/sulfur co-doped CQDs with a quantum yield of up to 73.1%. The generated CQDs might be used as fluorescent probes for the detection of uric acid [71]. Furthermore, by microwave heating a mixture of citric acid, boric acid, and urea, Bourlinos created nitrogen/boron co-doped green fluorescent CQDs [72]. Using hydroquinone as a carbon source and phosphorus tribromide as a phosphorus source, Zhou also created phosphorus-doped CQDs using the solvothermal approach. The resulting CQDs' fluorescence quantum yield was 25% [73]. In addition, the introduction of strongly tensile electron groups, such as –COOH, quaternary ammonium salt, -SeO or -SeO2, into CQDs by doping significantly reduces the singlet excited state (S1 energy level) of CQDs, causing their luminescence wavelength to move red to the near-infrared region. In addition, Li synthesized selenium-doped CQDs (Se-CQDs) by hydrothermal treatment of selenocysteine with a fluorescence quantum yield of 7.6%. It is shown that the selenium heteroatom confers redox-dependent reversible fluorescence in Se-CQDs [74].

In addition, adjusting the synthesis conditions can change the physical and chemical properties (particle size, surface chemical composition) of CQDs, and then regulate the energy level structure and photochemical properties of CQDs. On the one hand, to regulate the concentration of alkali in the synthesis process [75], the addition of oxidizing or reducing reagents can adjust the chemical composition of the surface, and then regulate the photochemical properties. Lan synthesized nitropyrene-sodium sulfite carbon dots (PyS-CDs) by hydrothermal synthesis using 1,3,6-trinitropyrene and Na2SO3 (λex = 490 nm, FL QY: 49%) [76]. Compared with Py-CDs (λex = 570 nm, FL QY: 4.9%) [77] synthesized with only 1,3,6-trinitropyrene, the addition of oxidizing sodium sulfite leads to an increase in oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of CQDs and an increase in fluorescence quantum yield during the carbonization process. At the same time, it destroys the large π-conjugate structure in CQDs, causes the fluorescence spectrum to shift blue, and weakens the photothermal conversion efficiency. On the other hand, changing the solvent system can adjust the size and optical properties of CQDs. Tian used citric acid and urea as precursors to perform hydrothermal synthesis in three different solvents (water, glycerol and dimethylformamide) to obtain CQD-water, CQD-GLY and CQD-DMF. Under transmission electron microscopy, the particle size of CQD-water, CQD-GLY, CQD-DMF can be observed gradually increasing. Ultraviolet and fluorescence spectra show a gradual redshift in emission wavelengths from blue to red. The researchers deduced the extent to which the solvent controlled the dehydration and carbonization process of the precursor, resulting in the formation of conjugates of different sizes extending blue to red [78]. In addition, increasing the concentration of NaOH during the reaction increases the particle size of the synthesized CQDs, the fluorescence wave is redshifted, the fluorescence quantum yield increases, and the fluorescence lifetime is shortened [79].

Photoluminescence is the most distinctive and fascinating optical characteristic of carbon dots. Photo luminescence is a phenomenon in which carbon dots obtain energy through the irradiation of external light, and then their energy levels transition to emit fluorescence (Fig. 3 ). Until now, no researchers have come up with an accurate photoluminescence mechanism for carbon dots [56]. There are mainly two existing light-emitting mechanisms, one is based on the band gap transition in the conjugated π domain, and the other is the surface defects of CQDs [80].

Some studies relate the red-shift of the emission of CQDs to quantum size effects using the first explanation. That is, they will display particle size-dependent PL behavior when the size of CQDs is smaller than their exciton radius [82]. Li used a one-step alkali-assisted electrochemical method to prepare CQDs with a size of 1.2–3.8 nm. The photoluminescence (PL) properties of CQDs with different sizes were significantly different. CQDs between 1.5 and 3.0 nm are visible light emissions, and CQDs with particle size >3.0 nm were near-infrared light emission [5]. Subsequently, Jiang created photoluminescence (PL) CQDs, which with a single UV light stimulation produced intense and steady red, green, and blue fluorescence. These PL emission alterations were caused by their different particle sizes [6]. In most cases, the PL spectra of CQDs inconspicuously exhibit quantum size effects but show a strong dependence on their chemical composition and structure [83].

In the latter mechanism, the surface state is the main factor affecting the fluorescence change. The surface state means that the luminescence phenomenon of CQDs is related to surface functional groups [84,85]. Surface functional groups can generate various energy potential traps, and both sp2 and sp3 hybridized regions can lead to surface energy traps [86]. This surface state defect-derived fluorescence of CQDs is attributed to the localized π-electron structure of electron-hole pairs in the sp2 state. Doped CQDs' luminescence mechanism is primarily linked to surface state luminescence. The fluorescence quantum yield of CQDs can thus be greatly increased through doping [87,88]. Zhang used the DMOL (3) approach, which is based on density functional theory, to discuss the interaction between carbon cores and surface groups. It was discovered that as the dose of the silane coupling agent was raised, the PL intensity and QY of CQDs also rose (KH-792). Additionally, it was hypothesized that doped N atoms were the primary source of the CQDs' high fluorescence emission. As a result, modifications to surface groups have a significant effect on the optical characteristics of CQDs [89]. Besides, Ran investigated the origin of the bimodal emission characteristic of bimodal emitting carbon dots (B-CDs). It is shown that under thermal induction, carbonyl-related surface-state PL centers were generated on the surface of B-CD, and the coexistence of carbonyl-related surface-state and core-state PL centers results in bimodal PL emission [19]. Furthermore, using hydrothermal synthesis to create C-dots-160 and C-dots-200 at temperatures of 160 °C and 200 °C, Fang investigated the connection between the microstructure and fluorescence emission behavior of C-dots. It was discovered that when the synthesis temperature rose, more oxygen and nitrogen atoms were added, increasing the overall number of structural flaws and altering their ratio of concentration. Therefore, C-dots-200 has stronger fluorescence emission [90].

In addition, some of the carbon dots have the characteristics of up-conversion photoluminescence [91,92], which is a phenomenon in which light with a shorter wavelength is released after successively absorbing two or more photons with a longer wavelength. CQDs with upconversion photoluminescence properties (long excitation wavelengths have better tissue penetration and molecular imaging capabilities) are easier to achieve in vivo imaging.

Researchers currently uses CQDs extensively, and the entry tickets are their excellent biocompatibility and low toxicity [2]. Mandal [93] used fluorescence imaging(FL) and photoacoustic imaging(PA) to investigate the distribution of P and N co-doped CQDs (P, N-CQDs) after injection (1 mg/mL, 100 μL) into male BALB/c mice. After injection, P, N-CQDs were uniformly distributed in the mouse body first and then enriched in the tumor site of the mouse through blood circulation. This research conclusion shows that CQDs have excellent application performance in the field of biological tumor imaging. Besides, Mewada [94] prepared NCDs with water chestnut peel extract in an attempt to reveal the biocompatibility of NCDs on MDCK cells. They found that these NCDs exhibited more than 80% cell viability at all concentrations (1−4 μg mL−1). In addition, CQD was used to fabricate bone scaffold to enhance bone mineralization and built-in co-monitoring [95–97]. The obtained CQDs were applied as fluorescent probes to monitor hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) in human teeth [98]. These studies showed that CQDs had low cytotoxicity [99,100] and high biocompatibility [101]. Therefore, CQDs can be widely used in biomedical fields such as biological imaging, bioanalytical detection, and drug delivery.

Bioimaging lays the foundation for the study of cell biology and developmental biology. Various fluorescent materials including organic dyes, semiconductor quantum dots, and upconversion nanoparticles have been developed for bioimaging. However, these materials are unsuited for general usage in the biomedical industry because to their high cytotoxicity, poor photostability, and inadequate biocompatibility [27]. CQDs not only overcome some shortcomings of traditional organic dyes and nanomaterials but have more superior characteristics than traditional semiconductor quantum dots, including small molecular weight and particle size, high fluorescence stability, no light flicker, wide excitation spectrum and continuous, tunable emission wavelength, good biocompatibility, low toxicity, etc. [102] It is easy to realize surface functionalization, which makes CQDs more and more widely used in cell imaging [103], in vivo imaging [104], bacterial imaging [105] and other fields(Fig. 4 ). In addition, the unique optical properties and carrier properties of CQDs make them widely used in photoacoustic imaging, photothermal imaging, and radionuclide imaging.

CQDs have been used extensively in cancer cell imaging. Generally, it has been demonstrated that CQDs can rapidly enter cells through pinocytosis, clathrin, caveolin mediator, or lipid-mediated endocytosis. Due to the difference in CQDs structure and cell type, CQDs can be distributed in mitochondria [107], lysosomes [106,108], endoplasmic reticulum [108,109], Golgi apparatus [110,111]or nucleolus [40,112].

Wu [13] prepared nitrogen-doped fluorescent carbon dots by hydrothermal method using tetraphenylporphyrin and its metal complexes as precursors to image HeLa cells. Under the excitation light of 405 nm, 458 nm, and 514 nm, it exhibited blue, green, and red fluorescence respectively. The fluorescence images revealed that CQDs were primarily dispersed in the cytoplasm and that the nucleus only displayed marginal fluorescence. Therefore, the CQDs were considered to enter the cell through endocytosis.

Further, Geng retro synthesized tunable fluorescent CQDs with citric acid and m-aminophenol, which were successfully used for mitochondria-targeted imaging of HeLa cells in vitro. The tracking time was 6 generations, which can be suitable for long-term cell imaging [113]. It had also been reported that CQDs had been applied to live cell nuclei and lysosome targeted imaging of HeLa cells, and co-localized well with commercial nuclei and lysosome trackers [112,114]. CQDs can enter the subcellular structure of cancer cells and can continuously monitor their biochemical processes, which is of great significance to diseases related to organelle damage and dysregulation.

Bacterial infection has always been one of the biggest killers of human health [115], therefore the pathological diagnosis of bacteria and the development of antibacterial drugs are very important. The fluorescence intensity and spectral position of CQDs depend on bacterial species, so in the case of mixed bacterial populations, fluorescent CQDs can be used as a tool to distinguish bacteria and further as fluorescent probes to label bacteria. Currently, CQDs have been applied to the imaging of microbial cells such as Escherichia coli, Staphylococcus aureus, yeast, Bacillus subtilis, etc. [116–118].

CQDs can withstand strong acid, produce obvious acidophilic fluorescence, and produce different fluorescence under different conditions; this characteristic can be used to distinguish Porphyromonas gingivalis, Streptococcus mutans, Escherichia coli, and golden yellow These four bacteria are staphylococcus. The pH of these four bacteria is different: Streptococcus mutans can produce acid during its metabolism, which can create an acidic environment; Porphyromonas gingivalis is more suitable for an alkaline environment, and it will release alkaline substances during its metabolism. Escherichia coli is a gram-negative bacterium, and its cell wall does not contain teichoic acid. While Staphylococcus aureus is a gram-positive bacterium, its cell wall contains teichoic acid, resulting in a difference in pH between the two bacteria. So if you image these four bacteria with CQDs, Streptococcus mutans can see a clear chain structure, while Porphyromonas gingivalis has only an unclear outline, E. coli can emit bright green light, and Staphylococcus aureus can emit bright red light [119].

Additionally, in vivo imaging using CQDs is possible [120]. It is important to note that as compared to the majority of green or yellow fluorescent CQDs, CQDs whose emission maximum is located in the near-infrared region have the advantages of reduced autofluorescence interference, deeper carbon dot marking on biological tissues, and less damage [121,122]. In recent years, it is the research hotspot to develop near-infrared emitting fluorescent CQDs with excellent properties and be used as contrast agents for cell imaging and in vivo imaging [123,124].

Li synthesized red fluorescent CQDs emitting in the near-infrared II region as a contrast agent by watermelon juice for in vivo optical imaging. The research results showed that under the excitation of 808 nm laser, the CQDs produced a red fluorescence emission peak at 900–1200 nm, which can realize near-infrared region II optical imaging in mice. It is worth noting that nearly 65% of CQDs can be excreted through mouse urine within 6 h, indicating that the kidneys can quickly remove CQDs from the body. In addition, the CQDs also had high photothermal efficiency (30.6%) under 808 nm light excitation, which was an ideal photothermal material for cancer treatment [125]. Hua also generated Ni2+-mediated CQDs with red fluorescence emission and various fluorescence quantum yields using a hydrothermal approach to realize real-time, wash-free, high-resolution nuclear imaging and high-contrast imaging of tumor sites in mice and zebrafish and can obtain STED images with resolution up to 146 nm [126].
In addition, CQDs, with their unique properties, have applications in photoacoustic imaging (PAI) and radionuclide diagnosis and treatment.

Photoacoustic imaging (PAI) mainly uses objects to absorb specific light waves and then generate ultrasonic waves, and then use acoustic detectors to absorb ultrasonic waves to form images. Ultrasound is able to penetrate thicker substances and can therefore overcome the limitations of optical imaging [127]. The large amino acid mimicking CQDs (LAAM CQDs) synthesized by Li can be accepted by acoustic detectors to produce images when irradiated with a laser light of 650 nm. In addition, the results showed that fluorescence signal intensity increased with increasing concentrations of LAAM TC-CQDs in the range of 2–10 μg ml−1 45.

CQDs can increase their temperature under laser irradiation, showing good photothermal performance. The photothermal conversion efficiency of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots synthesized by Das reached 52% [128]. However, this characteristic of CQDs is generally used for photothermal therapy guided by photothermal imaging, and the temperature of the target site is detected by an infrared thermal imager to judge the treatment status [129]. The photothermal therapy will be described in detail later.

In nuclear medicine, radionuclides are used primarily for diagnosis and therapy. Generally speaking, radionuclides mainly enter the relevant lesions of the human body through oral or intravenous administration and continuously emit radiation. They are then used with single photon emission computed tomography (SPECT)cameras or positron emission computed tomography (PET) phases for tomographic detection and imaging [130]. Stephan used 64Cu to label CQDs and then used PET to collect the distribution of Cu-NOTA-CQD in mice. The results demonstrated that the distribution information obtained was reliable and comparable to the data obtained with Wistar rats [131]. Although CQDs are used as carriers to transport radionuclides to target locations, there are still problems to be solved in practical applications, such as the energy range of radionuclides, which need to be scanned by corresponding cameras; The half-life should be only a few hours, depending on how precisely and quickly the CQDs can reach their destination [130].

Wang [133] reported a test strip based on double-emitting fluorescent CQDs (red and blue) for the semi-quantitative visual detection of Pb2+, and the detection limit was as low as 2.89 nmol/L. In an 8:1 ratio, blue fluorescent CQDs and red fluorescent CQDs were combined to create the sensing test strip. Pb2+ was added, which reduced the blue fluorescence while mostly maintaining the red fluorescence. The test strip's color progressively changed from blue to red as the concentration of Pb2+ rose, enabling the semi-quantitative detection of Pb2+. It was worth mentioning that the detected color RGB values could be identified using a smartphone, which provided a new idea for instant visual inspection in the field. In addition, it also realized a simple and intuitive detection of Pb2+ in actual water samples. The paper-based smartphone application analysis device proposed in this study could provide new ideas for the high-sensitivity determination of actual environmental samples, and further promote the on-site determination of actual samples.

Similarly, Sun [136] prepared three carbon quantum dot-metal ion (Ce3+, Fe3+, Cu2+) complexes as array sensors by pyrolyzing carbonized citrate formamide solution. Ce3+, Fe3+, and Cu2+ could induce CQDs to form aggregates of different degrees, resulting in different degrees of fluorescence quenching. The degree to which the CQD aggregates were depolymerized after the addition of phosphate anions depended on the degree of complexation between the various phosphate anions and the three metal ions. These resulted in different degrees of fluorescence recovery, thus achieving five Array recognition discrimination of phosphate anions. Another study found that the degree of fluorescence quenching varied depending on the bacteria's affinity for various receptor-modified CQDs. For example, Zheng constructed an “on-off” type fluorescent sensor to detect pathogenic bacteria [209].

A fluorescent sensor for DNA detection based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between CQDs and gold nanoparticles was published by Gao [183]. The peptide nucleic acid caused the gold nanoparticles to aggregate in the absence of DNA, blocking the FRET interaction between the CQDs and gold nanoparticles and allowing the system's fluorescence intensity to return. The gold nanoparticles, however, remained dispersed because the peptide nucleic acid was able to target the DNA when it was present and form a combination with it. This made it possible for the gold nanoparticles and CQDs to efficiently FRET, dimming fluorescence and enabling the measurement of DNA content. High sensitivity and a small detection limit were characteristics of the fluorescence sensor (0.21 nmol/L).

Besides, Zhang [195] prepared a pH fluorescence sensing test paper by CQDs and realized the qualitative and quantitative detection of pH values in a wide range. The CQDs could also be used as a new type of cell imaging indicator for detecting intracellular pH value, which had broad application prospects in the field of biomedicine.

CQDs can also be used as biosensors due to their low toxicity, good biocompatibility, high stability, and photoluminescence. Li [210] introduced pyrophosphate and high-energy phosphate bonds into C-dots as nuclear growth inhibitors and energy donors, respectively, and then used the synthesized C-dots as fluorescent probes to design H2O2-related biosensors. When Fe2+ and H2O2 were coupled, the fluorescence of C-dots was gradually quenched with the increase of H2O2 concentration. Therefore, when the sensor detects glucose, the glucose will be specifically oxidized by GOD (oxidase) to release H2O2, and then the fluorescence of C-dots will be gradually quenched. At the same time, they also verified the feasibility of the sensor, using the biosensor and the glucose oxidase-peroxidase (GOD-POD) method to estimate the glucose content in human serum samples. The results showed that the two methods obtained. The results are consistent. CQDs can also be combined with aptamers. Xu [211] used aptamers to functionalize C-Dots, and then thrombin interacted with aptamers to form a sandwich structure with aptamer-functionalized silica nanoparticles to selectively detect Thrombin. Lin [212] avoided modification of CDs as fluorescent probes to design biosensors for the detection of organophosphorus pesticides in vegetables. Fe2+ in the biosensor will be oxidized to Fe3+ by H2O2, which will cause the fluorescence quenching of CDs, and when organophosphorus pesticides are present, the production of H2O2 will be kept, so that the CDs will not undergo fluorescence quenching. This kind of biosensor can be used for the detection of the total amount of organophosphorus pesticides in vegetables. Cheng [213] also prepared a biosensor for the detection of organophosphorus pesticides, but their sensor is a new type of visible light-driven self-pulverizing photoelectrochemical biosensor. The sensor uses TiO2 as the photoactive base material and N-CQDs as the photosensitizer. The response speed of the obtained NCQD/TiO2/ITO electrode is about 42 times that of the TiO2/ITO electrode. Therefore, when the sensor is in the detection solution, the activity of AchE is controlled. Inhibition reduces the photocurrent response of the sensor to detect organophosphorus pesticides.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of CQDs in drug delivery
ce:section.ce:para: A drug delivery system (DDS) is a device that can deliver a drug to a particular target within the body with preset release kinetics and duration while also enabling the drug to interact with the target in the intended way. Nanomaterials combined with drugs apply to tumor-targeted drug delivery, which can reduce the side effects of drugs to a certain extent. Additionally, poorly soluble medications' water solubility can be increased because to the nanoscale size of nanomaterials. The application of nanotechnology in DDSs can simultaneously deliver two or more drugs or different therapeutic modalities, transfer macromolecular drugs, and monitor drug locations using imaging agents on drug carriers [214]. CQDs also show unique advantages in drug delivery which can be used as Potent nano-antibiotic for intracellular pathogens [215]. Meanwhile, CQDs-conjugated drugs, such as microbial B, are made into multispectral nanofluorescent probes for For diagnostic imaging and targeted tumor therapy [216].

Recently, CQDs have attracted the interest of researchers for their promising drug delivery applications [217]. Reasons why CQDs can be applied in drug delivery systems: 1) the fluorescent properties of CQDs enable real-time tracking and sensing capabilities to support drug delivery; 2) CQDs as a hollow nanocarrier can realize high drug loading; 3) The surface chemical modification is flexible to achieve targeting; 4) CQDs is a safe and biocompatible contrast agent, which can effectively guide the release of drugs, especially water-insoluble drugs. Among them, the commonly used anticancer drugs are doxorubicin (DOX), 7-(3-bromopropoxy)-2-quinoline methylchlorobutyl ester (Qucbl), platinum drugs (Pt), curcumin (Cur), etc., as shown in the table. Targeted therapy, stimulus-responsive therapy, photodynamic therapy, and photothermal therapy, as well as immunotherapy and gene therapy, are described below. Table 3 summarizes the application of different CQDs in drug delivery.

One of the strategies to achieve the targeting of CQD is to modify the surface of CQD with targeted ligands, which can specifically bind to receptors that are overexpressed on cancer cell membranes [218]. Folic acid (FA) [219,220] and hyaluronic acid (HA) [221] are typical targeted ligands that specifically bind to overexpressed folic acid and CD44 receptors on a variety of tumor cells, respectively. And other special targeting molecules can also specifically bind to cancer cells. FA-modified carbon dots (FA-Gd @ CQDs), obtained by one-pot microwave-assisted pyrolysis of waste crab shells and transition metal ions Gd3+, have been shown to target folate receptor-positive HeLa cells. After loading DOX, the anti-tumor efficacy of FA-Gd@CQDs was observed in HeLa cells, which provides a good application prospect for their targeted cancer therapy [219].

Another strategy to make CQD well targeted is to adjust the edges of CQD to the structure of amino acids and glucose. Specific treatment of tumors is achieved by utilizing specific carrier transporters that are differentially expressed in cancer cells, typically including large neutral amino acid transporter 1(LAT1), serine, alanine, cysteine-preferred transporter 2, and glucose transporters [222,223]. Li synthesized 1,4,5,8-tetraaminoanthraquinone (TAAQ) and citric acid (CA) into tumor-targeted LAAMTC-CQDs by hydrothermal treatment. The structure of LAAMTC-CQDs resembles large amino acids and can selectively target tumor cells through hyper-expressed LAT1-mediated endocytosis. The chemotherapy effect of LAAMTC-CQDs loaded with the chemotherapy drug topotecan was significantly enhanced [43]. In addition, Zheng used d-glucose and l-aspartic acid to directly pyrolyze to synthesize novel CQDs (CQD-Asp) targeting brain cancer gliomas. In vivo fluorescence pictures of CQD-Asp injected through the tail vein, the fluorescence signal found at the glioma site is much larger than in healthy brains. The researchers speculate that the targeting function of CQD-Asp may stem from the formation of RGD (a tripeptide consisting of L-arginine, glycine, and L-aspartic acid, a common glioma targeting agent) like functional group in CQD margins prepared from d-glucose and L-aspartic acid, which results in high glioma targeting efficiency of CQD-Asp [224].

The tumor microenvironment differs from the normal physiological milieu in a number of ways, including low pH, significant reducibility, differential enzyme expression, high reactive oxygen species, and sensitivity to sound, light and heat, etc. [225]. These features offer good opportunities for designing stimulus-responsive diagnostic and therapeutic platforms [222]. Based on this, researchers have studied Various responsive CQDs drug delivery systems, including light responsive(Fig. 6 ) [40], pH responsive [201], reduction responsive, etc. [176,204] to achieve highly effective tumor targeted treatment.

Yuan prepared an environmentally friendly pH-responsive CQDs-DOX drug-loading system by heating milk and using the electrostatic effect to combine with DOX [202]. DOX was also placed onto hollow CQDs in a different investigation. CQDS-DOX was quickly absorbed by cells and released under pH control, according to in vitro tests. Feng loaded the prodrug of cisplatin on CQDs (CQDs Pt (IV)) and carried the anionic polymer on the surface (CQDs Pt (IV)@ PEG-(PAH/DMMA), which released positive CQD-Pt (IV) in a glutathione-rich tumor microenvironment to achieve the purpose of tumor treatment (Fig. 7 ) [206].

Based on their sensitivity to light and heat, the researchers widely used CQDs for photodynamic therapy (PDT) and photothermal therapy (PTT). PDT refers to the process of relying on a specific wavelength of light source to activate the photosensitizer (PS) in tumor tissue to produce biotoxic reactive oxygen species (ROS), which in turn oxidatively damage the tumor [226]. Photothermal therapy (PTT) is a treatment method in which photothermal agents (PTA) with high photothermal conversion efficiency are concentrated near tumor tissue, and light energy is converted into heat energy under external light sources (usually near-infrared light) to kill cancer cells [77]. CQDs can be used as carriers of PDT/PTT due to their good water dispersibility, biocompatibility, EPR effect, easy surface modification, and large π-conjugate structure [227].

Ji use polythiophene phenylpropionic acid (PPA) as a carbon source, and synthesize carboxyl-rich CQDs by hydrothermal method. Subsequently, the target agent folic acid (FA) and the photosensitizer Chlorin e6 (Ce6) were covalently linked to the surface of the CQDs with triethylene glycol (TEG) as the linker to form MCND. Experiments show that the maximum emission peak of MCND is at 670 nm, the quantum yield is 4.32%, the yield of 1O2 is 17.3%, and the photothermal conversion efficiency is 25.9%. In vitro PDT/PTT synergistic therapy showed that due to the targeting properties of folic acid that allow MCNDs to effectively target and penetrate into Hela cells, the ROS and heat generated can kill cancer cells [228].

Some studies have shown that CQDs can produce ROS under illumination or have high photothermal conversion efficiency, so they can also be used as PS/PTA [216,229]. Compared with traditional photosensitizers, CQDs solve the shortcomings of traditional PS with low water solubility, easy aggregation and poor tumor targeting ability. However, CQDs typically display low absorption coefficients in the red to NIR region, suggesting that PTT requires a higher laser irradiation power density [230]. Zhao obtained nitropyrene CQDs (Py-CQDs) by hydrothermal synthesis with 1,3,6-trinitropyrene and NaOH as precursors, which were used for photoacoustic imaging-guided PDT and PTT synergy in the treatment of mouse tumors. Experiments show that the 1O2 yield of Py-CQDs is 5.7%, OH∙ can be generated, and the photothermal conversion efficiency is as high as 73.5% [77]. Lan synthesized selenium-doped-polythiophene-benzylammonium salt CQDs (PBN–Se-CQDs) by hydrothermal synthesis using polythiophene (PT2) and diphenyl diselenide as precursors. It has a high photothermal conversion efficiency (58.2%) and can be used for fluorescence and photothermal imaging-guided PTT of tumor mice. Due to Se-doping, the energy level of CQDs triplet is less than the energy required to produce 1O2 (>0.98 eV). In addition, energy gap law can also generate heat, so PBN-Se-CQDs do not produce ROS [231]. GE synthesized GQDs by hydrothermal method with polythiophenebenzonium salt as a precursor for fluorescence imaging-guided PDT of tumor mice. Experiments show that GQDs have near-infrared fluorescence (λex = 680 nm, FL QY: 5.4%), 1O2 quantum yield of 130%, and photothermal conversion efficiency <10%. Based on the above experimental data, GQDs can be used as PS for efficient tumor treatment [232].

To improve PDT/PTT functionality, researchers have conducted extensive studies on metal-doped CQDs such as copper, lanthanides, etc. [233]. On the one hand, doping of metal elements can change the electron distribution and surface structure of CQD, thereby improving the absorption of light by the overall material [234]. On the other hand, the doping of metal elements enables CQDs to simulate the function of natural enzymes, promote the production of oxygen-active substances (ROS), and improve the anti-tumor effect of PDT therapy. Han synthesized Arg-Ag@Cu-DOX by hydrothermal synthesis of L-arginine (L-Arg), silver nitrate(AgNO3) and copper nitrate(Cu(NO3)2). The results showed that the doping of Ag increased the fluorescence intensity of the emitted light, and the doping of Cu(II) made the CQDs exhibit catalase activity, and the ROS produced significantly improved the anti-tumor effect of PDT treatment [235]. In addition, the loaded DOX is released under the protonation of the acidic environment, and the drug treatment effect is enhanced. Li synthesized nucleo-shell structural Au@CQDs by hydrothermal reaction of HAuCl4 and CQDs for PDT of tumor cells. Studies have shown that under 808 nm near-infrared excitation, Au@CQDs exhibit peroxide (POD) enzyme and glutathione oxidase (GSHOx) activity. In addition, Au@CQDs mediated the participation of glutathione (GSH) in the reaction, accelerating the production of ROS, showing a photothermal enhanced photodynamic therapeutic effect [236].

Immunotherapy for tumors aims to activate the body's immune system and kill cancer cells by relying on autoimmune functions. In recent years, nanoparticle-based immunotherapy has great potential in tumor treatment1. The surface of CQDs is functionalized by targeted molecules or antibodies, making them highly selective and specific for immune cells [237]. At the same time, targeted CQDs, as good immune agent carriers, can produce stronger and more durable immune responses than single immune agents. Hou modified Asp-Ala-Thr-Gly-Pro-Ala peptide, DOX and Fe3+ on the surface of CQDs, and encapsulated the angiotensin inhibitor losartan (LOS) inside CQDs to form nano-self-assembled bodies (PepAPCQDs@Fe/DOX-LOS), which can be used in synergistic chemotherapy and immunotherapy for cancer [238].

In addition, CQDs-based nanoenzymes mimic the structure and function of natural enzymes, leading to the production of oxygen-active substances (ROS) in biological systems and subsequent oxidative stress damage, thereby promoting immune activation. Typical natural enzymes typically include GSH oxidase(GSH-PX) [239], catalase(CAT) [240], or superoxide dismutase (SOD) [241]. ChA CQDs synthesized by hydrogenic acid (ChA) can induce ferrozois and activate the tumor immune microenvironment, thereby inhibiting tumor growth. ChA CQDs have been reported to have significant GSH oxidase-like activity to catalyze glutathione (GSH) to oxidized glutathione (GSSG). It induces iron death in HepG2 cells by promoting an unbalanced redox reaction. In vivo experiments have shown that ChA CQDs can enhance the infiltration of T cells, NK cells and macrophages in tumor tissues, transform immunologically cold tumors into hot tumors, and inhibit tumor growth [239].

Gene therapy refers to the introduction of foreign normal genes into target cells to correct or compensate for diseases caused by defective and abnormal genes to achieve therapeutic purposes. At present, genes are mostly delivered through various cationic liposomes [242], polymers [243], inorganic nanoparticles [244], DNA self-assembly structures [245] and virus [246]. CQDs are one of the inorganic nanoparticles. Using CQD as a delivery vehicle for genes not only has high transfection efficiency, but also enables image tracking. At present, CQDs are commonly used to deliver plasmid DNA (pDNA) and condensation small interfering RNA (siRNA) [247]. Wang synthesized HA-CQDs by microwave-assisted pyrolysis of hyaluronic acid, HA and polyethyleneimine (PEI). The gel delay assay showed that HA-CQDs efficiently condensed plasmid DNA through electrostatic interactions at low doses. Colocalization imaging experiments showed that plasmid DNA could be efficiently released into the cytoplasm to achieve efficient gene transfection [248].

In recent years, some studies have shown that CQDs can produce a large number of free radicals under light, such as hydroxyl radicals (•OH), superoxide anions (•O2−) and singlet oxygen (1O2), often as photosensitizers, as discussed earlier. But CQDs can also scavenge a variety of free radicals in vivo and in vivo. Li synthesized selenium-doped CQDs (Se-CQDs) by hydrothermal method. Electron spin resonance (ESR) spectroscopy detected that Se-CQD has strong hydroxyl radical (•OH) scavenging ability, which can protect cells from oxidative stress [74]. Rosenkrans1 also synthesized Se-CQDs. In vivo experiments have shown that Se-CQD can quickly accumulate in the kidneys and has strong free radical scavenging ability, which can be used for the treatment of acute kidney injury [249]. Xue used EDTA salt as raw material to prepare Cu and Zn bimetal doped CQDs (CuZn-CQDs) by calcination. Antioxidant activity experiments showed that CuZn-CQDs had dual activity close to that of natural SOD enzymes and CAT enzymes. This may be due to the presence of a bimetallic active center of Cu–O–Zn to improve the charge transport capacity of the carbon spots. The good antioxidant activity of CuZn-CQDs makes it applied to photocatalyze the elimination of ROS in cells [250]. Ma et al. using heavy oil and three SDA products as precursors, synthesized highly fluorescent multi-color CQDs by hydrothermal method which gives a reliable and repeatable industry possibility and may boost the applications of CQDs into reality [251].

In addition, CQDs have anti-tumor effects on cancer cells [252,253]. Luo synthesized carbon nanospheres using glucose as raw material for the specific treatment of prostate cancer. In vitro experiments have shown that carbon nanospheres can inhibit the growth of prostate cancer cells and induce their death, with no effect on prostate epithelial cells. In vivo experiments have also confirmed that carbon nanospheres can inhibit tumor growth [254]. However, the interaction of carbon nanomaterials and tumors has not yet been elucidated. Further research is needed to confirm.

Gene delivery is sometimes required for disease treatment, but viral vectors can increase the risk of carcinogenesis. Although general non-viral vectors are safer, they do not have the ability to track themselves. Therefore, CQDs gradually reveal their advantages in this field [255]. Cao [256] loaded the gene plasmid SOX9 (pSOX9) onto the synthesized CQDs, followed by cell transfection. The experimental results show that CQDs/pSOX9 nanoparticles can transfect the gene plasmid SOX9 into the cells, so that the fibroblasts show obvious cartilage differentiation, and CQDs can also use adjustable fluorescence to track and image the delivered gene plasmid. Liu [257] synthesized functionalized carbon dots (CQD-PEI) through one-step microwave-assisted pyrolysis of branched polyethyleneimine (PEI) and glycerol, and through appropriate pyrolysis time, CQD-PEI can not only enhance the photoluminescence of CQD, can also aggregate DNA as a polyelectrolyte. The results of in vitro transfection experiments showed that CQD-PEI can efficiently and accurately transfect COS-7 and HepG2 cells.

This paper systematically discusses the synthesis strategy, fluorescence properties, and luminescence mechanism of CQDs. Additionally, the development of CQDs in the biomedical area is highlighted, particularly the development in the four distinct disciplines of drug delivery, bioanalytical detection, bioimaging and biosensing. CQDs exhibit small particle size, good biocompatibility, easy surface functionalization, high fluorescence stability, and tunable emission wavelengths, thus gaining widespread development in the biomedical field. Despite the above-mentioned noteworthy advancements, additional study is still required to clear various obstacles in the field of biomedical applications.

Usually CQDs need to be further purified after being prepared, but there are still many disadvantages in the purification methods currently used: 1) The separation accuracy is not enough, which requires multiple methods to be combined, which makes the operation complicated; 2) The equipment is bulky, high cost; 3) It takes a long time and the separation efficiency is low. These problems are also a challenge for researchers. Solving these problems can promote the research and practical application of CQDs in basic fields.

The optical properties and low toxicity of CQDs determine that they are more suitable for biological imaging than traditional organic dyes and nanoparticles, but the emission wavelength of CQDs is mainly limited to the blue-green light region, which can only perform shallow imaging, and deep imaging requires near-infrared light waves. However, the photostability and yield of near-infrared CQDs are urgent problems [88]. Researchers in the future need to actively explore how to prepare new CQDs with stable and high yields that emit near-infrared light. In terms of analysis and detection, it is necessary to continuously improve the selectivity, sensitivity, and accuracy of sensors or detection equipment, and at the same time, it is necessary to further study the mechanism of sensor fluorescence quenching, so as to avoid the autofluorescence of CQDs. The design of drug delivery systems using CQDs has always been a hot topic, but current research in this area is basically limited to the experimental animal stage, and there are still challenges described above in clinical translation. In addition, in addition to the applications described above, the drug delivery system can also combine the multiple functions of CQDs to achieve multifunctional targeted therapy, drug delivery and PDT/PTT while imaging in vivo.

In addition, CQDs are also a hot research direction in the field of catalysis. On the one hand, it is used as a photocatalyst to degrade pollutants, and on the other hand, it is used to catalyze the hydrogen evolution reaction in the energy field. Because different reactions require different catalysts, functionalized carbon quantum dots must be synthesized accordingly. However, the photoelectric properties and structure of the synthesized functionalized quantum dots are still unclear, and researchers need to conduct further research on their electronic structure, catalytic active center, and functional group interaction in the future. For CQDs, this is an urgent problem not only in the field of catalysis but also in other applications. If these obstacles can be overcome, CQDs may be applied more widely in biomedical research.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Ms Hai-Li Yang received her B.S. degree in Chemistry from the Qingdao University (Qingdao, China) from 2017 to 2021. She is currently a graduate student at the School of Life Sciences, Nanjing University. Her research interests include fluorescent tracer drugs and drug delivery.

Dr. Li-Fei Bai received her B.S. and Ph.D. degrees at the School of Chemical, Jilin University (Changchun, China) in 2001 and 2006 (major in Analytical Chemistry). She was a postdoctoral fellow at Nanjing University of Chinese Medicine from 2006 to 2009 and began with research work on the screening of active ingredients of traditional Chinese Medicine and anticancer drug screening studies. She became Lecturer in 2011 in Jiangsu Second Normal University. Her work focuses on medicinal chemistry.

Dr. Zhi-Rong Geng received the B.S. and M.S. degrees in Veterinary medicine from Nanjing Agricultural University (Nanjing, China) in 2000 and 2003, respectively. He received his Ph.D. degree at the School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Nanjing University (Nanjing, China), in 2009 (major in Bioinorganic Chemistry and Chemical Biology). He was a postdoctoral fellow at Nanjing University from 2009 to 2012 and began with research work on chemical biology and anticancer drug screening studies. He became Researcher in 2012 in the State Key Laboratory of Coordination Chemistry, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Nanjing University. Now he is a faculty member of College of Pharmacy of Nanjing University of Chinese Medicine. His work focuses on chemical biology and medicinal chemistry.

Mr. Chen Hao received a B.S. degree in Engineering from Hefei University of Technology (Hefei, China) from 2017 to 2021. He is currently a graduate student in the School of Life Sciences, Nanjing University. His research interests include quantum dots as drug carriers to deliver drugs in the body, and quantum dots combine with anti-cancer drugs to play a therapeutic role.

Ms Letian Xu received a B.S. degree in Pharmaceutical Engineering from Northwest A&F University (Shanxi, China) 2017 to 2021. She is currently a graduate student at the School of Life Sciences, Nanjing University. Her research interests include the study of peptide-coupled drugs in cardiovascular and cerebrovascular diseases and the combination of drugs for leukemia and treatment.

Dr. Yang-Cun Xie studied at Hohai University from 2002 to 2006 and received her bachelor's degree in environmental engineering. From 2006 to 2009, she majored in Environmental Science at Hohai University and obtained her master's degree. From 2009 to 2012, she studied at China University of Geosciences (Beijing), majoring in Environmental Science and Engineering, and received her PhD degree. From 2015 to now, she has been engaged in research in the field of water quality target management in the Chinese Academy of Environmental Planning. She won the title of associate researcher in 2020.

Dr. Dong-Jin Wang received his B.S. degree in Norman Bethune Health Science Center of Jilin University from 1981 to 1986 and received cardiovascular surgery training for ten years. He received his Ph.D. degree in Nanjing University of Traditional Chinese Medicine in 2011. Now, he is the chief of the Department of Cardiothoracic Surgery in Nanjing Drum Tower Hospital, the director of the Jiangsu Great Vascular Disease Diagnosis and Treatment Center and the principle investigator of the Institute of Cardiothoracic Vascular Disease in Nanjing University. He is also one of the most famous cardiovascular surgeons in China. His major research interest is the mechanism of aortic dissection, atrial fibrillation and myocardial infraction, and the translational research of cardiovascular diseases.

Dr. Hong-Wei Gu graduated from Nanjing Agricultural University (Nanjing, China) from 1997 to 2001. He received his Ph.D. degree at the College of Veterinary Medicine, Nanjing Agricultural University (Nanjing, China) at 2007. Then, he joined School of Life Sciences as a postdoctoral fellow at Nanjing University from 2007 to 2009 and started research on infection and immunity. He worked as Lecturer in 2009 in the State Key Laboratory of Pharmaceutical Biotechnology, School of Life Science, Nanjing University. In this period, he mainly focuses on the study of non-coding RNA regulation, infection and immunity, and anti-cancer molecular targeted anti-Tumor agents. 2016–2018, He entered into Iowa State University as visiting faculty (research associate) and further researched on infection and immunity. 2018, He joined the Nanjing Integrated Traditional Chinese and Western Medicine Hospital as High-Level Talents.

Dr. Xiao-Ming Wang received his B.S. degree in Chemistry from the Inner Mongolia Normal University (Hohhot, China) from 1997 to 2001. He received his Ph.D. degree at the School of Chemical, Jilin University (Changchun, China), from 2001 to 2006 (major in Organic Synthesis, Medicinal Chemistry, and Glycochemistry). He was a postdoctoral fellow at Nanjing University from 2006 to 2008 and began with research work on chemical biology of synthesis and anticancer drug screening studies. He became Lecturer in 2008 in the State Key Laboratory of Pharmaceutical Biotechnology, School of Life Science, Nanjing University. His work focuses on medicinal chemistry, chemical biology, and the total synthesis of natural products.
